content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


June 6, 2002



Diana D. Halpenny, General Counsel
San Juan Unified School District
Legal Services Department
Post Office Box 477
Carmichael, CA 95609-0477

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-110

Dear Ms. Halpenny:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the San Juan
Unified School District regarding the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as an
evaluation of any conduct that may already have taken place. Further, this letter is based
on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does not act as the
finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Under the facts presented, would the publication by the San Juan School District
in its district newspaper of articles about school activities and the names of school board
members who attended the activities constitute a violation of the mass mailing
prohibition in the Act?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Under the facts below and unless an exception to the mass mailing
prohibition applies, the school district's newspaper, as proposed, would be a prohibited
mass mailing under Government Code section 89001.

___________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear in Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-02-110
Page No. 2

FACTS

The San Juan Unified School District's newspaper is published in part by district
funds and in part through advertising revenues. It is mailed monthly to all employees of
the school district and homes of all students and various community members. It exceeds
two hundred copies. It often highlights various events and ceremonies at the various
school sites throughout the district, and you would like to note board members who were
in attendance. You request advice whether doing so will violate the mass mailing
prohibition in the Act.

ANALYSIS

Section 89001 states that "[n]o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed) implements and interprets section
89001 and provides that a mailing is prohibited if all of the following four criteria are
met:

¶. . .

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by an means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For
purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must
be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written
document.
(2)    The item sent either:
(A)    Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, or
(B)    Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer.
(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or
(B)    Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are
paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is done
with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.
(4)    More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a
single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b)."
(Regulation 18901(a).)

1.    Definition of Mass Mailing and Unsolicited Requests

Regulation 18901 only applies to a "mass mailing" as defined by the Act. "Mass
mailing" means more than 200 substantially similar pieces of mail in a single calendar
month, but does not include mail that is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter

File No. A-02-110
Page No. 3

or other inquiry, or any items that fall within the exceptions set forth in Regulation
18901(b).² (Regulation 18901(a)(4).) Thus, materials sent in response to an unsolicited
request are not subject to the mass mailing prohibition. Regulation 18901(c) provides
that "[a] person who subscribes to newspapers or other periodicals published by persons
other than elected officers shall be deemed to have made unsolicited requests for
materials published in those subscription publications." (Regulation 18901(c)(4)(E).)

You have stated that the school district's newspaper is mailed monthly to all
employees of the school district and the homes of all students and various community
members. Therefore, the newspaper does not constitute a subscription publication. You
have indicated that the number of newspapers mailed monthly exceeds 200 copies.
Accordingly, the school district's newspaper falls within the definition of "mass mailing"
for purposes of regulation 18901.

2.     Delivery

     Regulation 18901 only restricts tangible items that are mailed or delivered, by any
means, to a person's home, office, or post office box. The district's newspaper is mailed
to all employees of the school district and various community members, presumably to
the employees' or community members' homes, offices, or post office box addresses, and
to the homes of all students. Therefore, the district's newspaper is "delivered" as that
term is used in regulation 18901.

3.     Public Moneys

     Regulation 18901 specifies that a mass mailing is sent at public expense within
the meaning of section 89001 if either of two events occurs: 1) any of the costs of
distribution are paid for with public moneys; or 2) more than $50 in public moneys is
used to pay for the costs of design, production, and printing, and this activity is done with
the intent to send the mailing other than as permitted by the regulation. (Regulation
18901(a)(3).)

     We have advised that even if the production and distribution of a newsletter is
funded only in part with public funds, the mailing must comply with section 89001.
(Battery) Advice Letter, No. A-94-303.) According to your facts, the school district's
newspaper is funded in part by district funds and in part through advertising revenues.
Therefore, the school district's newspaper meets at least one of the public funding tests of
subpart (a)(3) of regulation 18901.

4.     Inclusion of a Reference to an Elected Officer

     If a mailing "[i]ncludes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected

² Please note that exceptions to the mass mailing prohibition applicable to your facts as presented are
considered below.

File No. A-02-110
Page No. 4

officer", the mailing is subject to the prohibition of section 89001. (Regulation
18901(a)(2)(B).) An elected official is affiliated with an agency if the official is a
member, officer or employee of the agency or a subunit thereof such as a committee of
that agency; has supervisory control over the agency; or appoints any of the members of
the agency. (Regulation 18901(c)(1); Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-89-096.) Therefore,
if the newspaper is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with the elected officer, any use of the elected officer's name, photograph or office, or
any reference to the officer is prohibited. We have interpreted this language very
broadly. We have advised that a mailing is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination or concert with an elected officer if the elected officers affiliated with an
agency are in any way involved with the production or distribution of the items sent.
This means, for example, that the elected officers affiliated with the agency may not
review the newspaper or make suggestions for articles to be included." (Culver Advice
Letter, No. A-90-140.) If the elected officer reviews or provides information for the
newsletter, that would be considered cooperating, consulting, coordinating, or preparing
and sending in concert with the elected officer, and the inclusion in the school district's
newspaper of the district school board member's name would meet this requirement.

5.        Exceptions to Regulation 18901

Regulation 18901(b) describes 11 exceptions to the mass mailing prohibition;
some of these exceptions may apply to some of the newspapers you propose to mail.
For example:

Regulation 18901 (b)(3) provides :

"Any item sent in the normal course of business from one governmental
entity or officer to another governmental entity or officer."

Regulation 18901 (b)(4) provides another exception to the mass mailing
prohibition:

"Any intra-agency communication sent in the normal course of business to
employees, officers, deputies, and other staff."

In short, if newspapers are mailed to other governmental entities or officers or to
employees, officers, deputies, and other staff of the school district in the normal course of
business, the mailing of those items does not count toward the 201 mark for mass
mailing. If, however, the number of items mailed which are subject to the mass mailing
prohibition exceeds 200 copies, the mailing would constitute a mass mailing subject to
the limitations of Government Code section 89001.

File No. A-02-110
Page No. 5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

Kelly L. Winsor

By: Kelly L. Winsor, Legal Analyst
Legal Division

Enclosure: Regulation 18901

in its district newspaper of articles about school activities and the names of school board
members who attended the activities constitute a violation of the mass mailing
prohibition in the Act?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Under the facts below and unless an exception to the mass mailing
prohibition applies, the school district's newspaper, as proposed, would be a prohibited
mass mailing under Government Code section 89001.
